explanation: The requirement appears to be that prop_0 (possibly an event like 'off-on sequence') must occur in the absence of prop_1 (possibly a fault or interlock). So, whenever prop_0 happens, at that precise step, prop_1 must be false. This is globally expressed as: whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 does not hold.
The statement requires that prop_0 never happens without prop_1. In LTL, this is that globally, prop_0 implies prop_1.
output_LTL: G ((failSafeMode & (PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode)) -> (off & powerButton))

explanation:
The specification says 'Fail-safe Mode: The prop_0 without a prop_1 (turn off and then turn on the machine)'. Interpreting this: we want whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 does not hold at that moment, which expresses 'prop_0 without a prop_1'. Additionally, 'turn off and then turn on the machine' implies a sequence: first the system is off, then on, perhaps in response to these signals. To capture the central requirement on the propositions provided, we assert that it's never the case that prop_0 and prop_1 are true at the same time ('fail-safe'):
Globally: !(prop_0 & prop_1).

The natural language statement requires that 'fail-safe mode' occurs in states where prop_0 holds and prop_1 does not hold. So, expressing 'the prop_0 without a prop_1' in LTL translates to: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must not hold at that moment.

The statement suggests that for the system to be fail-safe, prop_0 should occur without prop_1, which means that 'turning off' (prop_0) happens when 'turning on' (prop_1) does not. This can be represented as 'globally, whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 does not hold at that moment.'
output_LTL: G((failSafeMode -> !(PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode | ventilating)) -> !((off & powerButton) -> !off))

explanation: The sentence specifies that the event prop_0 (the controller does prop_0) is only allowed if prop_1 is true at that time. In other words, it's always the case that prop_0 does not occur unless prop_1 is also true. This can be expressed as globally (G), if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 must be true.
output_LTL: G ((failSafeMode & (PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode)) -> (off & powerButton))

explanation: The requirement is that, once the controller is in mode prop_0, it cannot leave prop_0 unless a prop_2 occurs first. This means: if we are at prop_0 and will eventually leave prop_0 (go to not prop_0), then before reaching not prop_0, prop_2 must occur. This can be represented by stating that whenever we are at prop_0 and will leave it, then prop_2 must happen before leaving. In LTL, this can be written as: G( prop_0 & X( !prop_0 ) -> ( prop_0 U ( prop_2 & X( !prop_0 ) ) ) )

The statement suggests that fail-safe mode is defined by a sequence: first, 'prop_0' (turn off) is true, and then, before 'prop_1' (turn on) occurs, 'prop_0' must happen. This can be interpreted as: whenever a turn on (prop_1) happens, it must be preceded by a turn off (prop_0).
output_LTL: G ((powerButton & off) -> F ((failSafeMode -> !(PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode)) & (!(powerButton & off) U (failSafeMode -> !(PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | StartUpMode)))))

explanation: This specification requires that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' does not hold at the same time. This can be captured by stating that globally, it is not the case that both 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' are true at once.
output_LTL: G (!((failSafeMode -> !(PSVMode | PCVMode | SelfTestMode | StartUpMode | StandbyMode | ventilating)) & (off & powerButton)))

explanation: The requirement states that the 'fail-safe mode' is characterized by the condition where 'prop_0' holds while 'prop_1' does not. In LTL, this condition can be expressed as: whenever the system is in 'prop_0', then 'prop_1' is not true at that moment. Globally (G), whenever 'prop_0' holds, '!prop_1' must hold as well.
